approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Check every possible pair of buy and sell days by iterating through the array with nested loops to find the maximum profit. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Maximum Profit Calculation
ladders.desc: Complete nested loop solution to find maximum profit from stock prices
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to the 'Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock' problem. The intuition is straightforward: to maximize profit, we need to find the largest difference between a later selling price and an earlier buying price. Since we can only buy once and sell once, we need to examine every possible pair of days where we could buy on day i and sell on day j (where j > i). The brute force approach does this by using two nested loops: the outer loop iterates through all possible buying days, and the inner loop iterates through all possible selling days that come after the buying day. For each pair, we calculate the profit (prices[j] - prices[i]) and track the maximum value encountered. The time complexity is O(n²) because for each of the n buying days, we examine up to n selling days. Space complexity is O(1) since we only use a few variables. This approach is inefficient for large inputs but serves as a foundation for understanding the problem structure. Common mistakes include: 1) Forgetting to ensure j > i, 2) Not handling edge cases like empty arrays or single-element arrays, 3) Initializing max_profit incorrectly (should be 0, not negative infinity, since we can choose not to trade). The connection to the parent problem is direct - this is the complete implementation of the brute force approach. Real code pattern: def maxProfit(prices): max_profit = 0; for i in range(len(prices)): for j in range(i+1, len(prices)): profit = prices[j] - prices[i]; if profit > max_profit: max_profit = profit; return max_profit

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Nested Loop Profit Calculator
ladders.desc: Calculate maximum profit using nested loops for a given price array
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the core nested loop structure that examines all possible buy-sell pairs. The intuition is that to find the maximum profit, we need to compare every possible combination of buying on day i and selling on day j where j > i. The algorithm uses two nested loops: the outer loop (i) represents potential buying days, and the inner loop (j) represents potential selling days that occur after the buying day. For each pair (i, j), we calculate the profit as prices[j] - prices[i] and update a maximum profit variable if this profit exceeds the current maximum. Time complexity is O(n²) where n is the number of days, as we have n iterations of the outer loop and up to n iterations of the inner loop. Space complexity is O(1) since we only use constant extra space. This sub-routine connects to the full problem as it contains the complete logic for finding maximum profit - the full problem just wraps this with proper initialization and edge case handling. Common mistakes include: 1) Starting the inner loop at i instead of i+1 (which would allow selling on the same day), 2) Not resetting profit calculations correctly, 3) Using wrong comparison operators. The key insight is that we must ensure j > i to maintain the temporal constraint of buying before selling. Real code pattern: max_profit = 0; for i in range(len(prices)): for j in range(i+1, len(prices)): profit = prices[j] - prices[i]; if profit > max_profit: max_profit = profit

ladders.title: Core Logic — Comparing All Buy-Sell Pairs
ladders.desc: Generate and evaluate all possible (buy_day, sell_day) combinations where sell_day > buy_day
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental combinatorial logic of examining all valid buy-sell pairs. The intuition is that maximum profit must come from one of the possible (i, j) pairs where i < j (buy on day i, sell on day j). We need to systematically generate all such pairs and evaluate their profit. The algorithm conceptually creates all combinations of two indices where the second index is greater than the first. For n days, there are n×(n-1)/2 such pairs. The step-by-step process is: 1) Start with buy_day = 0, 2) For each buy_day, consider sell_day from buy_day+1 to n-1, 3) Calculate profit for each pair, 4) Track maximum profit. Time complexity is O(n²) because we examine approximately n²/2 pairs. Space complexity is O(1) for the tracking variables. This core logic connects to the nested loop implementation by providing the conceptual framework that the loops implement. The key insight is that we're essentially doing a complete search of the solution space - this is why it's brute force. Common mistakes in understanding this logic include: 1) Thinking we need to consider selling before buying, 2) Missing that we need to consider ALL pairs, not just adjacent days, 3) Not understanding why there are n×(n-1)/2 total pairs. Real code pattern: for buy_day in range(n): for sell_day in range(buy_day+1, n): evaluate profit

examples.trace:
Array has 4 elements, indices 0-3
All valid pairs (buy_day, sell_day):
(0,1): buy at 2, sell at 5 → profit = 3
(0,2): buy at 2, sell at 1 → profit = -1
(0,3): buy at 2, sell at 3 → profit = 1
(1,2): buy at 5, sell at 1 → profit = -4
(1,3): buy at 5, sell at 3 → profit = -2
(2,3): buy at 1, sell at 3 → profit = 2

Maximum profit among all pairs: 3 (from pair 0,1)

Array has 4 elements, indices 0-3
All valid pairs (buy_day, sell_day):
(0,1): buy at 4, sell at 3 → profit = -1
(0,2): buy at 4, sell at 2 → profit = -2
(0,3): buy at 4, sell at 1 → profit = -3
(1,2): buy at 3, sell at 2 → profit = -1
(1,3): buy at 3, sell at 1 → profit = -2
(2,3): buy at 2, sell at 1 → profit = -1

All profits are negative, so maximum profit = 0

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Through Array Pairs
ladders.desc: Generate all index pairs (i, j) where j > i for an array
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on generating all ordered pairs of indices where the second index is greater than the first. The intuition is that many problems require comparing elements in different positions, and this pattern of generating (i, j) pairs with i < j is fundamental. The algorithm uses nested loops: the outer loop variable i goes from 0 to n-2 (since j must be at least i+1), and the inner loop variable j goes from i+1 to n-1. This generates exactly n×(n-1)/2 pairs, which is the number of ways to choose 2 distinct ordered elements from n items. Time complexity is O(n²) as we generate approximately n²/2 pairs. Space complexity is O(1) if we just generate and process pairs without storing them. This connects to the core logic by providing the mechanism to generate the buy-sell day pairs. The key insight is understanding the loop boundaries: i stops at n-2 because when i = n-1, there are no j > i; j starts at i+1 to ensure temporal ordering. Common mistakes include: 1) Starting j at i instead of i+1 (which would include same-day pairs), 2) Going beyond array bounds, 3) Not understanding why there are n×(n-1)/2 total pairs. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(arr)-1): for j in range(i+1, len(arr)): process pair (i, j)

examples.trace:
n = 3
Outer loop i from 0 to 1 (n-2):
  i=0:
    Inner loop j from 1 to 2:
      j=1 → pair (0,1)
      j=2 → pair (0,2)
  i=1:
    Inner loop j from 2 to 2:
      j=2 → pair (1,2)

Total pairs: 3 = 3×2/2

n = 4
Outer loop i from 0 to 2 (n-2):
  i=0:
    Inner loop j from 1 to 3:
      j=1 → pair (0,1)
      j=2 → pair (0,2)
      j=3 → pair (0,3)
  i=1:
    Inner loop j from 2 to 3:
      j=2 → pair (1,2)
      j=3 → pair (1,3)
  i=2:
    Inner loop j from 3 to 3:
      j=3 → pair (2,3)

Total pairs: 6 = 4×3/2

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Element Difference Calculation
ladders.desc: Calculate the difference between two array elements at different indices
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves subtracting the value at an earlier index from the value at a later index in an array. The intuition is simple: if we think of array elements as stock prices on different days, then prices[j] - prices[i] where j > i represents the profit from buying on day i and selling on day j. The algorithm is straightforward: given an array 'arr' and two indices i and j where j > i, compute arr[j] - arr[i]. This operation has O(1) time complexity and O(1) space complexity. It connects to the building block by being the computation we perform on each generated pair. The key insight is understanding that this difference represents gain/loss when moving from position i to position j. Common mistakes include: 1) Subtracting in wrong order (i - j instead of j - i), 2) Not ensuring j > i, 3) Accessing indices outside array bounds. This operation is fundamental to many array comparison problems beyond just stock trading. Real code pattern: difference = arr[j] - arr[i]

examples.trace:
arr = [5,8,3,10]
i = 0 → arr[i] = 5
j = 2 → arr[j] = 3
difference = arr[j] - arr[i] = 3 - 5 = -2

arr = [2,7,4,9]
i = 1 → arr[i] = 7
j = 3 → arr[j] = 9
difference = arr[j] - arr[i] = 9 - 7 = 2

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Simple Subtraction
ladders.desc: Access array elements by index and perform basic arithmetic subtraction
ladders.explanation: This is the most fundamental concept required: accessing elements in an array using indices and performing subtraction between two numbers. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, and we can access any element using its position (index). Arrays are typically 0-indexed, meaning the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, etc. To access an element, we use the syntax arr[index]. Subtraction is one of the basic arithmetic operations: a - b gives the difference between a and b. If a > b, the result is positive; if a < b, the result is negative; if a = b, the result is zero. Time and space complexity are both O(1) for these operations. This foundation connects to the basic operation by providing the tools needed: we need to access two array elements and subtract them. Common beginner mistakes include: 1) Confusing 0-indexing with 1-indexing, 2) Trying to access indices outside the array bounds, 3) Not understanding that array indices must be integers, 4) Confusing subtraction with other operations. The key insight is that arrays provide random access to elements, and subtraction computes the difference between values. Real code pattern: element1 = arr[i]; element2 = arr[j]; result = element2 - element1

examples.input: Array: [10,20,30], access index 1, then index 2, subtract first from second
examples.trace:
Array: [10,20,30]
Access index 1: arr[1] = 20
Access index 2: arr[2] = 30
Subtract: arr[2] - arr[1] = 30 - 20 = 10

examples.input: Array: [5,3,7], access index 0, then index 1, subtract first from second
examples.trace:
Array: [5,3,7]
Access index 0: arr[0] = 5
Access index 1: arr[1] = 3
Subtract: arr[1] - arr[0] = 3 - 5 = -2

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a dynamic programming approach by tracking the minimum price seen so far and updating the maximum profit at each step. This mimics Kadane's algorithm for maximum subarray but adapted for stock prices.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Profit Stock Trading with DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find maximum profit from one buy/sell transaction using dynamic programming approach.
ladders.explanation: This problem asks us to find the maximum profit possible from one buy and one sell transaction given historical stock prices. The DP approach works by maintaining two key variables as we iterate through prices: the minimum price seen so far (min_price) and the maximum profit achievable so far (max_profit). At each price point, we first update min_price to be the minimum between current min_price and current price. Then we calculate potential profit by subtracting min_price from current price, and update max_profit if this potential profit is greater. This works because for any selling day, the optimal buying day must be the day with minimum price before that selling day. By tracking the minimum price encountered, we can compute the best profit for each potential selling day in O(1) time. The algorithm mimics Kadane's algorithm but adapted for profit calculation rather than subarray sums. Time complexity is O(n) since we make one pass through the array, and space complexity is O(1) as we only use two variables. Common mistakes include initializing min_price incorrectly (should be first price or infinity), forgetting to update min_price before calculating profit, or trying to buy and sell on same day (profit should be 0, not negative). The connection to L1 is that this full solution implements the complete profit calculation logic described there.

examples.trace:
Initialize min_price = 7, max_profit = 0
Day 0: price = 7, min_price = min(7,7) = 7, profit = 7-7 = 0, max_profit = max(0,0) = 0
Day 1: price = 1, min_price = min(7,1) = 1, profit = 1-1 = 0, max_profit = max(0,0) = 0
Day 2: price = 5, min_price = min(1,5) = 1, profit = 5-1 = 4, max_profit = max(0,4) = 4
Day 3: price = 3, min_price = min(1,3) = 1, profit = 3-1 = 2, max_profit = max(4,2) = 4
Day 4: price = 6, min_price = min(1,6) = 1, profit = 6-1 = 5, max_profit = max(4,5) = 5
Day 5: price = 4, min_price = min(1,4) = 1, profit = 4-1 = 3, max_profit = max(5,3) = 5
Final answer: 5

Initialize min_price = 7, max_profit = 0
Day 0: price = 7, min_price = min(7,7) = 7, profit = 7-7 = 0, max_profit = max(0,0) = 0
Day 1: price = 6, min_price = min(7,6) = 6, profit = 6-6 = 0, max_profit = max(0,0) = 0
Day 2: price = 4, min_price = min(6,4) = 4, profit = 4-4 = 0, max_profit = max(0,0) = 0
Day 3: price = 3, min_price = min(4,3) = 3, profit = 3-3 = 0, max_profit = max(0,0) = 0
Day 4: price = 1, min_price = min(3,1) = 1, profit = 1-1 = 0, max_profit = max(0,0) = 0
Final answer: 0

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Profit Calculation with Minimum Tracking
ladders.desc: Core function that calculates maximum profit by tracking minimum price while iterating.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements the heart of the DP solution: maintaining minimum price and calculating maximum profit during iteration. The intuition is that for any selling price, the optimal buying price is the minimum price encountered before that point. By tracking the minimum price seen so far, we can compute the best possible profit for each selling opportunity in constant time. The algorithm initializes min_price to the first price (or a very large number) and max_profit to 0. Then for each subsequent price: 1) Update min_price = min(min_price, current_price), 2) Calculate potential_profit = current_price - min_price, 3) Update max_profit = max(max_profit, potential_profit). This works because we're essentially asking: 'If I were to sell today, what's the best profit I could get by buying at the lowest price seen before today?' Time complexity is O(n) for n prices, space is O(1). Connection to L2: This implements the core logic of tracking and updating two variables. Common mistakes include updating min_price after calculating profit (should be before), or initializing min_price to 0 (should be first price or infinity). The real pattern is a single-pass iteration with two state variables.

examples.trace:
Initialize: min_price = 3, max_profit = 0
Price 3: min_price = min(3,3)=3, profit=3-3=0, max_profit=max(0,0)=0
Price 8: min_price = min(3,8)=3, profit=8-3=5, max_profit=max(0,5)=5
Price 1: min_price = min(3,1)=1, profit=1-1=0, max_profit=max(5,0)=5
Price 4: min_price = min(1,4)=1, profit=4-1=3, max_profit=max(5,3)=5
Final: 5

Initialize: min_price = 5, max_profit = 0
Price 5: min_price=min(5,5)=5, profit=5-5=0, max_profit=max(0,0)=0
Price 2: min_price=min(5,2)=2, profit=2-2=0, max_profit=max(0,0)=0
Price 9: min_price=min(2,9)=2, profit=9-2=7, max_profit=max(0,7)=7
Price 1: min_price=min(2,1)=1, profit=1-1=0, max_profit=max(7,0)=7
Price 7: min_price=min(1,7)=1, profit=7-1=6, max_profit=max(7,6)=7
Final: 7

ladders.title: Core Logic — Two-Variable State Tracking
ladders.desc: Maintaining and updating two key variables (min_price and max_profit) during iteration.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core technique of maintaining two state variables while processing a sequence. The intuition is that many optimization problems can be solved by tracking just a couple of key values that represent the best solution found so far and some supporting information. For stock profit, we need to know: 1) The minimum price seen (to determine best buying opportunity), and 2) The maximum profit achievable so far (our answer). As we process each new price, we update both variables: min_price becomes the minimum of itself and current price (tracking best buying price), and max_profit becomes the maximum of itself and (current_price - min_price) (tracking best selling opportunity). This is efficient because we only need O(1) memory regardless of input size. Time complexity for processing n items is O(n). Connection to L3: This builds on the concept of comparing and updating values. Common mistakes include updating variables in wrong order (must update min_price before calculating profit) or forgetting to initialize properly. The pattern is a template for many DP problems where optimal solution depends on maintaining certain statistics during iteration.

examples.input: Track min and max profit for prices: [4,9,2,8]
examples.trace:
Start: min_price=4, max_profit=0
Process 4: min_price=min(4,4)=4, profit=4-4=0, max_profit=max(0,0)=0
Process 9: min_price=min(4,9)=4, profit=9-4=5, max_profit=max(0,5)=5
Process 2: min_price=min(4,2)=2, profit=2-2=0, max_profit=max(5,0)=5
Process 8: min_price=min(2,8)=2, profit=8-2=6, max_profit=max(5,6)=6
Final state: min_price=2, max_profit=6

examples.input: Track min and max profit for prices: [6,3,7,1,5]
examples.trace:
Start: min_price=6, max_profit=0
Process 6: min_price=min(6,6)=6, profit=6-6=0, max_profit=max(0,0)=0
Process 3: min_price=min(6,3)=3, profit=3-3=0, max_profit=max(0,0)=0
Process 7: min_price=min(3,7)=3, profit=7-3=4, max_profit=max(0,4)=4
Process 1: min_price=min(3,1)=1, profit=1-1=0, max_profit=max(4,0)=4
Process 5: min_price=min(1,5)=1, profit=5-1=4, max_profit=max(4,4)=4
Final state: min_price=1, max_profit=4

ladders.title: Building Block — Finding Minimum in Sequence with Updates
ladders.desc: Tracking the minimum value encountered while iterating through a sequence.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves maintaining the minimum value seen so far while processing elements one by one. The intuition is simple: as we encounter new values, we compare them with our current minimum and update if the new value is smaller. This is fundamental to the stock problem because the best buying price is the minimum price before any selling point. The algorithm: initialize min_so_far to the first element (or a very large number), then for each subsequent element, set min_so_far = min(min_so_far, current_element). This ensures min_so_far always holds the smallest value encountered up to that point. Time complexity is O(n) for n elements, space is O(1). Connection to L4: This builds on basic comparison operations. Common mistakes include initializing min_so_far to 0 (wrong for positive numbers) or forgetting to update. This pattern appears in many algorithms where we need to track extreme values (min/max) during processing. In the stock problem context, this minimum tracking gives us the best possible buying price at any point.

examples.trace:
Start: min_so_far = 5
Element 5: min_so_far = min(5,5) = 5
Element 3: min_so_far = min(5,3) = 3
Element 8: min_so_far = min(3,8) = 3
Element 2: min_so_far = min(3,2) = 2
Element 7: min_so_far = min(2,7) = 2
Final minimum: 2

Start: min_so_far = 10
Element 10: min_so_far = min(10,10) = 10
Element 15: min_so_far = min(10,15) = 10
Element 8: min_so_far = min(10,8) = 8
Element 12: min_so_far = min(8,12) = 8
Element 6: min_so_far = min(8,6) = 6
Element 9: min_so_far = min(6,9) = 6
Final minimum: 6

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Element Comparison and Value Update
ladders.desc: Comparing two values and updating a variable based on the comparison result.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves comparing two values and conditionally updating a variable. In programming, this typically takes the form: if (new_value < current_min) then current_min = new_value, or using min() function: current_min = min(current_min, new_value). The intuition is that we're maintaining a 'best so far' value that improves when we find something better. For minimum tracking, 'better' means smaller; for maximum tracking, 'better' means larger. This operation is atomic and appears in countless algorithms. Time complexity is O(1) per comparison, space is O(1). Connection to L5: This builds on understanding variables and comparison operators. Common mistakes include using wrong comparison operator (>< confusion) or forgetting to actually update the variable. In the stock problem context, we use this to update min_price when we find a lower price, and to update max_profit when we find a higher profit. The pattern is fundamental to optimization algorithms where we iteratively improve a solution.

examples.trace:
Compare: 3 < 5 is true
Update: min = 3 (since 3 is smaller)
Result: min changed from 5 to 3

Compare: 6 > 8 is false
Update: max stays 8 (since 6 is not larger)
Result: max remains 8

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Variables and Simple Arithmetic
ladders.desc: Understanding variables, assignment, and basic arithmetic operations.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers the basics of variables and arithmetic that underpin the entire solution. A variable is a named storage location that holds a value. We can assign values to variables (e.g., min_price = 7) and update them (e.g., min_price = 5). Basic arithmetic operations include addition (+), subtraction (-), multiplication (*), and division (/). For the stock problem, the key operations are subtraction (profit = sell_price - buy_price) and comparison (checking if one value is less than or greater than another). The intuition is that we're storing and manipulating numerical values to compute results. Time and space analysis isn't applicable here as these are primitive operations. Connection to L4: This provides the building blocks for comparison and update operations. Common beginner mistakes include confusing assignment (=) with equality comparison (==), or incorrect order of operations. Understanding that variables can change value during program execution is crucial for the iterative updates in the stock algorithm. This level assumes no prior programming knowledge beyond basic mathematics.

examples.trace:
1. Store buy_price as 10
2. Store sell_price as 15
3. Calculate profit = sell_price - buy_price = 15 - 10
4. Result: profit = 5

1. Store current_min as 8
2. Store new_price as 6
3. Compare: is new_price < current_min? 6 < 8 is true
4. Update: current_min = new_price = 6
5. Result: current_min is now 6

approaches.name: Two Pointer Greedy O(n)
approaches.summary: Maintain two pointers for buy and sell days, moving them greedily to maximize profit by adjusting based on current prices. This avoids nested loops and uses a sliding window-like technique.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Maximum Profit with Two Pointer Greedy
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find maximum profit from one buy/sell transaction using two pointers.
ladders.explanation: This problem asks us to find the maximum profit achievable from one buy and one sell transaction given daily stock prices. The Two Pointer Greedy approach solves this optimally in O(n) time with O(1) space by maintaining two pointers that represent potential buy and sell days. The intuition is that we want to buy at the lowest price seen so far and sell at the highest price after that buy point. We initialize buy pointer at day 0 and sell pointer at day 1. As we iterate through the array, we update the buy pointer whenever we find a lower price (since buying lower gives better potential profit). We calculate profit at each step by comparing current price with the buy price, updating max profit if we find a better value. The algorithm works because we're essentially tracking the minimum price seen so far (buy pointer) and calculating profit against each subsequent price (sell pointer). Step-by-step: 1) Initialize buy = 0, max_profit = 0. 2) For sell from 1 to n-1: a) If prices[sell] < prices[buy], update buy = sell (found better buy price). b) Else calculate profit = prices[sell] - prices[buy], update max_profit if profit > max_profit. 3) Return max_profit. Time complexity is O(n) since we traverse array once. Space is O(1) using only pointers. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of updating pointers based on price comparisons. Common mistakes include not handling empty arrays, not initializing max_profit to 0, or trying to sell before buying. Real code pattern: def maxProfit(prices): buy = 0; max_profit = 0; for sell in range(1, len(prices)): if prices[sell] < prices[buy]: buy = sell else: profit = prices[sell] - prices[buy]; max_profit = max(max_profit, profit); return max_profit

examples.trace: Initialize: buy=0 (price=7), max_profit=0. sell=1 (price=1): 1<7 → update buy=1. sell=2 (price=5): 5>1 → profit=5-1=4 → max_profit=4. sell=3 (price=3): 3>1 → profit=3-1=2 → max_profit stays 4. sell=4 (price=6): 6>1 → profit=6-1=5 → max_profit=5. sell=5 (price=4): 4>1 → profit=4-1=3 → max_profit stays 5. Return 5.

Initialize: buy=0 (price=7), max_profit=0. sell=1 (price=6): 6<7 → update buy=1. sell=2 (price=4): 4<6 → update buy=2. sell=3 (price=3): 3<4 → update buy=3. sell=4 (price=1): 1<3 → update buy=4. No profit calculated since prices never increase after buy. Return 0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Pointer Update Based on Price Comparison
ladders.desc: The core logic of updating buy/sell pointers when finding better prices.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine handles the decision-making process of when to update our buy and sell pointers. The intuition is that we want to maximize profit by always buying at the lowest price seen so far and selling at the highest price after that buy. The algorithm: We maintain two indices - buy (potential buy day) and current (current day being considered). At each step, we compare prices[current] with prices[buy]. If prices[current] < prices[buy], we update buy = current because we found a lower price to buy at. Otherwise, we calculate potential profit = prices[current] - prices[buy] and track the maximum. This works because updating buy to a lower price resets our potential profit calculation - we can't sell before buying, so when we find a new minimum, all previous calculations become irrelevant. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(1) per comparison. Space is O(1). Connection to L0: This is the decision logic executed at each iteration in the full solution. Connection to L2: This implements the greedy choice of always taking the lowest buy price. Common mistakes include updating buy pointer incorrectly when prices are equal (shouldn't update since no benefit), or trying to calculate profit before ensuring sell day > buy day. Real code pattern: if prices[current] < prices[buy]: buy = current else: profit = prices[current] - prices[buy]; max_profit = max(max_profit, profit)

examples.trace: Compare prices[1]=3 with prices[0]=5. 3<5 → Found lower price. Update buy pointer from 0 to 1. No profit calculation since we updated buy.
Compare prices[3]=7 with prices[2]=4. 7>4 → Can calculate profit. profit = 7-4 = 3. Compare with current_max=2. 3>2 → Update max_profit to 3. buy pointer stays at 2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Greedy Choice of Minimum Buy Price
ladders.desc: The insight of always tracking the minimum price seen so far for maximum profit potential.
ladders.explanation: This core logic captures the fundamental insight that to maximize profit with one transaction, we should always buy at the lowest price encountered so far. The intuition: Profit = Sell Price - Buy Price. For any given sell day, the best buy day is the day with the minimum price before that sell day. Therefore, as we iterate through prices, we maintain the minimum price seen so far. At each new day, we calculate potential profit using current price as sell price and the minimum price so far as buy price. This greedy approach works because it considers all possibilities optimally - for each potential sell day, we use the best possible buy day (the minimum before it). The algorithm: Initialize min_price = first price, max_profit = 0. For each price in array: 1) Update min_price = min(min_price, current_price). 2) Calculate profit = current_price - min_price. 3) Update max_profit = max(max_profit, profit). Time O(n), space O(1). Connection to L1: This is why we update buy pointer when finding lower prices. Connection to L3: This builds on comparing and tracking minimum values. Common mistakes include initializing min_price incorrectly (should be first price, not 0 or infinity), or calculating profit before updating min_price (should update min first). Real code pattern: min_price = prices[0]; max_profit = 0; for price in prices: min_price = min(min_price, price); profit = price - min_price; max_profit = max(max_profit, profit)

examples.trace: Initialize: min_price=3, max_profit=0. price=8: min_price=min(3,8)=3; profit=8-3=5; max_profit=5. price=1: min_price=min(3,1)=1; profit=1-1=0; max_profit stays 5. price=5: min_price=min(1,5)=1; profit=5-1=4; max_profit stays 5. Return 5.
Initialize: min_price=10, max_profit=0. price=9: min_price=min(10,9)=9; profit=9-9=0. price=8: min_price=min(9,8)=8; profit=8-8=0. price=7: min_price=min(8,7)=7; profit=7-7=0. max_profit remains 0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Tracking Minimum Value in Array
ladders.desc: Finding and maintaining the minimum value while iterating through an array.
ladders.explanation: This building block teaches how to track the minimum value in an array during a single pass. The intuition is simple: as we examine each element, we compare it with the current minimum and update if we find a smaller value. This is fundamental to many algorithms including our stock problem. Algorithm: Initialize min_val = first element (or a very large number if array might be empty). For each element in array: if element < min_val, update min_val = element. Time O(n), space O(1). Connection to L2: This is exactly what we do with min_price - tracking minimum price seen so far. Connection to L4: This uses comparison operations between elements. Common mistakes include not handling empty arrays, initializing min_val to 0 (which might not be in array), or forgetting to include first element in comparison. Real code pattern: if array empty return error; min_val = array[0]; for val in array: if val < min_val: min_val = val

examples.trace: Initialize min_val=5. val=2: 2<5 → min_val=2. val=8: 8<2? No → min_val stays 2. val=1: 1<2 → min_val=1. val=9: 9<1? No → min_val stays 1. Return 1.
Initialize min_val=10. val=20: 20<10? No. val=30: 30<10? No. min_val remains 10.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation of comparing two numerical values.
ladders.explanation: This is the most basic operation needed: comparing two numbers to determine which is smaller, larger, or if they're equal. The intuition is straightforward - we need to compare prices to make decisions about buying and selling. In programming, comparison operators (<, >, <=, >=, ==, !=) return boolean values. For our stock problem, we primarily use less-than (<) to find lower prices and greater-than (>) implicitly when calculating profit. Algorithm: Given two numbers a and b, we can: 1) Check if a < b (a is less than b). 2) Check if a > b (a is greater than b). 3) Check if a == b (a equals b). These comparisons form the basis of decision-making in our algorithm. Time O(1), space O(1). Connection to L3: Tracking minimum requires comparing each new value with current minimum. Connection to L5: This builds on understanding numbers and their relationships. Common mistakes include confusing < with <=, or not considering edge cases like negative numbers (though prices are typically positive). Real code pattern: result = a < b  # returns True or False

examples.output: a < b is True, a > b is False, a == b is False
examples.trace: Compare 5 and 8. 5 < 8 is True because 5 is less than 8. 5 > 8 is False. 5 == 8 is False.

examples.output: a < b is False, a > b is False, a == b is True
examples.trace: Compare 10 and 10. 10 < 10 is False (not strictly less). 10 > 10 is False. 10 == 10 is True (they are equal).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of arrays and how to access elements by index.
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers what arrays are and how to work with them. An array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by an index (position). In our stock problem, the prices are stored in an array where each index represents a day (0 = day 1, 1 = day 2, etc.). Key concepts: 1) Arrays are zero-indexed (first element at index 0). 2) We can access elements using array[index]. 3) Arrays have length (number of elements). 4) We can iterate through arrays using loops. For example, if prices = [7,1,5], then prices[0] = 7, prices[1] = 1, prices[2] = 5. The length is 3. This is fundamental because our algorithm needs to examine each price by its position (day). Time to access an element by index is O(1). Space depends on array size. Connection to L4: We need arrays to store prices that we compare. Connection to real problem: Without understanding arrays, we couldn't represent daily prices. Common mistakes include accessing indices beyond array bounds (e.g., index 3 in array of length 3), or misunderstanding zero-indexing. Real code pattern: arr = [10,20,30]; first = arr[0]; second = arr[1]; length = len(arr)

examples.trace: Array has elements: index 0 = 5, index 1 = 8, index 2 = 3. Accessing index 1 gives the second element, which is 8.

examples.input: Array: [10,20,30,40], Get length and last element
examples.trace: Array has 4 elements. Length is 4. Last element is at index 3 (since zero-indexed: 0=10,1=20,2=30,3=40). So last element = 40.
